heading: Alert Conditions
body:
You can configure the following alert conditions in the project-level
[Configure Alert Settings](configure-alerts) page to trigger alerts.

[Atlas Search: Index Replication Lag](<Atlas Search: Index Replication Lag is>) occurs if the approximate
number of milliseconds that MongoDB Search is behind in replicating changes from
the [oplog](manual/reference/glossary) of mongod is above or below the threshold.

If replication lag grows large enough that `mongot` falls behind the configured oplog
window, `mongot` can no longer sync incrementally. When this happens, `mongot` will
automatically perform a full index rebuild. During the rebuild, search queries continue to
work but will return stale results.

To reduce replication lag:

- Increase your oplog size to give `mongot` more time to sync during write bursts.
  You can configure the minimum oplog size in your [Set Oplog Size](set-oplog-size).

- Upscale your Search Nodes to give `mongot` more CPU and memory for replication. To
  learn more about adding or scaling Search Nodes, see [Tips for Sizing and Scaling Search Nodes](fts-search-node-sizing-tips).

- Reduce write burst volume by batching or throttling large bulk operations when possible.

- Monitor write volume alongside replication lag. A sudden spike in write throughput is a
  common trigger for oplog exhaustion. To learn more about monitoring write volume and issue
  resolution, see [Fix Oplog Issues](replication-oplog-alerts).

**Recommended threshold:** Set this alert well below your oplog window duration. For
example, if your oplog window is 24 hours, consider alerting at 30–50% of that window
(e.g., 8–12 hours) to give yourself time to respond before `mongot` falls off the oplog.

[Atlas Search: Index Size on Disk](<Atlas Search: Index Size on Disk is>) occurs if the total size of all
MongoDB Search indexes on disk in bytes is above or below the threshold.

[Atlas Search: Mongot paused initial sync](<Atlas Search: Mongot paused initial sync>) occurs if initial sync
is interrupted by the MongoDB Search `mongot` process due to high disk
utilization.

[Atlas Search: Mongot is approaching replication stop threshold](<Atlas Search: Mongot is approaching replication stop threshold>)
occurs if disk usage on a search node is greater than or equal to 85%.

[Atlas Search: Max Number of Lucene Docs](<Atlas Search: Max Number of Lucene Docs is>) runs automatically by default.
This alert occurs if the upper bound number of Lucene docs used to store
MongoDB Search indexes for a given replica  set or shard is above the threshold.

[Atlas Search: Mongot stopped replication](<Atlas Search: Mongot stopped replication>) runs automatically by default.

This alert occurs only on dedicated Search Nodes when replication is
interrupted by the MongoDB Search `mongot` process due to high disk
utilization. Replication pauses when disk utilization reaches 90% and
resumes automatically when disk utilization falls below 85%.

When replication is paused, Search Nodes might continue serving queries,
but replicated data can become stale. For example, if `mongot` is
already running, synonym mappings might remain loaded but may not
reflect recent updates. If Atlas provisions a new Search Node while
replication is paused, the new mongot process might not have synonym
mappings available.

Paused replication can also delay or prevent fast search node
provisioning, which relies on Atlas replicating a recent copy of
your index when scaling Search Nodes.

If replication is paused for an extended period the `mongot` process
can fall off the oplog. If `mongot` falls off the oplog, Atlas
rebuilds the index.

To prevent `mongot` from falling off the oplog:

- Upscale your Search Nodes to reduce disk utilization.

- Delete unused MongoDB Search indexes.

- Add additional Search Nodes.

- Shard your cluster to better distribute data and reduce disk pressure.

[Atlas Search: Number of Error Queries](<Atlas Search: Number of Error Queries is>) occurs if the number of
queries for which MongoDB Search is unable to return a response is above or
below the threshold.

[Atlas Search: Number of Successful Queries](<Atlas Search: Number of Successful Queries is>) occurs if the number
of queries for which MongoDB Search successfully returned a response is above or
below the threshold.

[Atlas Search: Total Number of Queries](<Atlas Search: Total Number of Queries is>) occurs if the number of
queries submitted to MongoDB Search is above or below the threshold.

[Atlas Search Opcounter: Delete](<Atlas Search Opcounter: Delete is>) occurs if the total number of
documents or fields (specified in the index definition) removed per
second is above or below the threshold.

[Atlas Search Opcounter: Getmore](<Atlas Search Opcounter: Getmore is>) occurs if the total number of
`getmore` commands run on all MongoDB Search queries per second is above or
below the threshold.

[Atlas Search Opcounter: Insert](<Atlas Search Opcounter: Insert is>) occurs if the total number of
documents or fields (specified in the index definition) that MongoDB Search
indexes per second is above or below the threshold.

[Atlas Search Opcounter: Update](<Atlas Search Opcounter: Update is>) occurs if the total number of
documents or fields (specified in the index definition) that MongoDB Search
updates per second is above or below the threshold.

[Insufficient disk space to support rebuilding search indexes](<Insufficient disk space to support rebuilding search indexes>) runs
automatically by default. This alert occurs when your cluster
runs out of enough free disk space to support your MongoDB Search indexes.

> **Note:** This alert might appear when Atlas automatically [upgrades your search indexes](index-create-and-update) to enable new
> features. Your cluster must have sufficient disk
> space for both the previous and new version of the index. If the
> rebuild increases disk space use to 90% or more, Atlas increases
> the cluster storage if you enabled auto-scaling. After the index
> upgrade completes, Atlas deletes the old version of the index,
> which frees up disk space.

[Search Memory: Resident](<Search Memory: Resident is>) occurs
if the total bytes of resident memory occupied by the MongoDB Search process is
above or below the threshold.

[Search Memory: Shared](<Search Memory: Shared is>) occurs if
the total bytes of shared memory occupied by the MongoDB Search process is above
or below the threshold.

[Search Memory: Virtual](<Search Memory: Virtual is>) occurs if
the total bytes of virtual memory occupied by the MongoDB Search process is
above or below the threshold.

[Search Process: CPU (Kernel) %](<Search Process: CPU (Kernel) % is>) occurs if the percentage of time
the CPU spent servicing operating system calls for the MongoDB Search process is
above the threshold.

[Search Process: CPU (User) %](<Search Process: CPU (User) % is>)
occurs if the percentage of time the CPU spent servicing the MongoDB Search
process is above the threshold.

[Search Process: Disk space used](<Search Process: Disk space used is>)
occurs if the total bytes of disk space used by the MongoDB Search process is above the threshold.

[Search Process: Ran out of memory](<Search Process: Ran out of memory>)
runs automatically by default. You can configure the [Configure Alert Settings](configure-alerts)
to disable this notification. This alert occurs when the search process (`mongot`)
runs out of memory. When the search process runs out of memory, indexing and queries fail.
